I respect your opinion, though I must disagree with a number of points. I don't think the film necessarily needs to "solve the problem," as I think the film would be over far too fast if it did so. It exhibits how Carey Mulligan's character has been coping with and dealing with the death of her friend, and the rest of the plot plays out as a revenge thriller as that was the intent. The film isn't for everyone, but I found the way it blends drama and conveying a message with the thrill of revenge to be very satisfying and it worked incredibly well for me. The film ends out with Cassie's revenge desires fulfilled, and I'm okay with that because I still felt personally affected by the social themes from the rest of the film.
The best thing I can say is humor is subjective, but I've walked away from it twice now feeling underwhelmed.

Okay I lied there actually are some notes, I just wanted my review to reflect how I was feeling when the credits rolled…

Obviously there are “objectively better” movies out there but leave it to an experience like this, even factoring all the imperfections of the film itself, to remind me how formative this franchise was to me as a person and why I’m in the field I’m in. I stopped counting the full body chills after a certain point just so I could be present in the moment, but I swear the total has to have been at least like 7 lmao. The credits crawl, binary sunset, the Obi-Wan reveal, Alderaan, the trash compactor, the pursuit on the Death Star, the final trench run, being reminded that the 1997 CGI sticks out against the completely seamless models, paintings, and other practical effects, dude the MUSIC in surround sound… we all need experiences like this to reinvigorate us. This was fucking awesome and I’m so happy.

I do agree, especially in the moment last night, although after sleeping on it I think I was making a bit of a harsh generalization for all theaters. There are arthouse theaters with FAR better audience behavior, and even theaters like Alamo Drafthouse where they actually make an explicit point to kick out people who don’t know how to act. Granted, a bad audience is impossible to predict and can happen anywhere, but there are definitely safer options than chain theaters where general audiences vary so insanely greatly.

Long ass response lol but I think it also helped me come to terms with last night and how high in regard I’ve held the theatrical experience, since theaters have been a sacred space for me for the last 4 years.

Also -- would make for a fascinating double feature with Jeanne Dielman in that they are the complete opposites of each other. This being a huge New Wave and anti-establishment creature of chaos that just says fuck all to any sort of slow and/or sensical films, with Jeanne Dielman being an utterly slow and fully compliant creature of chaos that says fuck all to any sort of upbeat and/or hyperstylized films. They both have total ownership of their cinematic language in two very different ways, and they're both great. Loved nearly all the cinematography and editing in this. The faux silent film and scissors scenes stuck out to me the most, but it's hard to deny the power of everything involving the banquet. The psychedelic train sequences were awesome too. Not the most emotionally gripping thing ever, but all in all just very fun and a great watch to ease me back into watching movies after a weeklong break (thanks to both circumstances out of my control and occasionally my hesitance to turn something on). I honestly needed something as chaotic and singular as this to kick things off again, wild movies are so fun and cool and it's so sick to me that people find deep-rooted emotional connections to shit like this.
